courses.name: Introductory Philosophy: Fields, Figures and Problems
courses.description: What is metaphysics? Who was Socrates? What is Freedom? This  introduction sketches many branches of philosophy and the important problems associated with each. It introduces great philosophers, present and past, and traces enduring philosophical themes.

courses.name: Looking at Philosophy
courses.description: Introduction to philosophy: the nature of logical thinking; the existence of God; the objectivity of values; the meaning of life; free will, determinism and responsibility; the relation between mind and body; immortality; the possibility of knowledge.  This course is not intended for Majors.

courses.name: The Meaning of Life
courses.description: An introduction to concerns expressed by the perennial philosophical question, "What is the meaning of life?"  Students will be familiarized with the major philosophical approaches to life's meaning through a consideration of various contemporary and late modern works in the philosophy of life.

courses.name: Mind, World, and Knowledge
courses.description: Introduction to a variety of philosophical works, including contemporary, on such topics as: the nature of being, the mental, the external, consciousness, perception, experience, meaning, truth, the nature of knowledge, scientific understanding, and how language and thought represent the world.

courses.name: Contemporary Moral, Social and Religious Issues
courses.description: Moral theories, atheism or theism, feminism, and free will. Moral arguments concerning abortion, affirmative action, racism, human rights, children's rights, world hunger, capital punishment, euthanasia, censorship, pornography, legal paternalism, animal rights and environmental protection.

courses.name: Introduction to Ethics and Social Issues
courses.description: An introduction to understanding, assessing, and formulating ethical arguments concerning controversial issues. Particular issues studied may include, world hunger, capital punishment, terrorism, euthanasia, abortion, pornography and hate speech, animal rights, the environment, and topics in theories of race, gender and disability.

courses.name: Great Philosophical Ideas, Part 1
courses.description: Major figures and developments in philosophy from the early Greeks to the year 1400.  Descriptive and comparative approach, providing an understanding of the place of philosophers in the history of thought.  Appreciation of critical reasoning is included for comprehending philosophical developments.

courses.name: Great Philosophical Ideas, Part 2
courses.description: Major figures and developments in philosophy after the year 1400.  Descriptive and comparative approach, providing an understanding of the place of philosophers in the history of thought.  Appreciation of critical reasoning is included for comprehending philosophical developments.

courses.name: Philosophy of Love and Sex
courses.description: A survey of philosophical classics, on themes of romantic love, self-love, altruistic love, sexuality, eroticism and the passion/reason dichotomy, from Plato's Symposium to Foucault's History of Sexuality; and an examination of related contemporary issues in light of these perspectives.

courses.name: Introduction to Logic
courses.description: An introduction to the techniques and philosophical implications of formal logic with emphasis on translation of expressions into symbolic form, testing for logical correctness, the formulation and application of rules of inference, and the relation between logic and language.  Open to first-year students.

courses.name: Critical Thinking
courses.description: Assessment of reasoning and the development of cogent patterns of thinking. Reference to formal logic is minimal. Practice in criticizing examples of reasoning and in formulating one's own reasons correctly and clearly.  Open to first-year students.

courses.name: Ancient Philosophy:  The Search for Wisdom
courses.description: An exploration of ancient philosophy as a search for wisdom and happiness from its Presocratic beginnings in Greece to its development in the Hellenistic world and Imperial Rome. Emphasis on philosophy as a contemplative activity and as a way of life.

courses.name: Issues in Theoretical Philosophy
courses.description: Issues drawn from epistemology, metaphysics, philosophy of mind, philosophy of language, and related fields will be examined through careful study of significant philosophical texts after 1900, along with some ensuing debates.

courses.name: Issues in Practical Philosophy
courses.description: Issues drawn from ethics, social and political philosophy, and related fields will be examined through careful study of significant philosophical texts , along with some ensuing debates.

courses.name: History of Ethics
courses.description: An introduction to ethical theories through a study of some of the major figures in moral philosophy, such as Aristotle, Hume, Kant and Mill.

courses.name: Philosophy of Human Rights
courses.description: Philosophical introduction to human rights sources, concepts, justifications, consequences, and challenges to them. Evolution of selected human rights as a) demands made in political struggles; b) declarations supported by moral or political principles and arguments; c) codes ratified and implemented by governments and international organizations.

courses.name: Information Ethics
courses.description: An exploration of ethical issues that arise in the Age of Information. Topics to be discussed may include technology, surveillance and privacy, social media and privacy, social media and cognitive bias, bias in algorithms, AI ethics, intellectual property, and freedom of expression and assembly.

courses.name: Philosophy of Technology
courses.description: Philosophical investigations of the nature of technology and the influence it has on our relationships with others, the natural world, and ourselves. Key themes may include the relation between technology and science and the role of technology in personal identity, social justice, and well-being.

courses.name: Introduction to Marxist Philosophy
courses.description: The evolution of Marx's social and political views in the setting of 18 th - and 19 th - century anarchism, liberalism and conservatism. Themes of humanism, freedom, rights, the state, democracy, alienation, and inequality, primarily as they develop into the theory of historical materialism.

courses.name: Topics in Marxist Philosophy
courses.description: The dialectical materialism of Marx, Engels, and Lenin is compared with traditional materialist, idealist, and mechanist philosophy. Marxist views on issues such as equality, ethical objectivity, human well-being, matter and mind, the existence of God, knowledge versus skepticism, freedom of the will, and justice.

courses.name: Introduction to the Philosophy of Science
courses.description: Philosophical issues arising out of the attempt to understand the world scientifically. Topics may include: scientific methodology, revolution, observation, explanation, causation, induction, reduction, the difference between natural and social scientific understanding, realism, instrumentalism, constructivism.

courses.name: Philosophy and Feminism
courses.description: A study of philosophical issues arising from feminism. The course includes discussions of the historical roots of feminism, the role of reason and emotion, key concepts such as oppression, sexism, equality and difference, feminism and philosophies of race and of disability, and selected moral/political issues.

courses.name: Gender and Philosophy
courses.description: Topics may include gender and sex in the history of philosophy, intersections between the politics and theories of gender, sexuality, and race, the place of the body in philosophical theory, the influence of gender and sex on science/social science, and queer/trans issues and politics.

courses.name: Children, Literature, and Philosophy
courses.description: An exploration of issues at the nexus of philosophy, children’s literature, and childhood studies.  Topics may include an examination of children’s books and young adult literature through a philosophical lens, as well as a critical examination of the “philosophy with children” movement.

courses.name: Happiness, Well-being, and the Good Life
courses.description: A philosophical exploration of what makes a good human life. Topics may include the role of happiness, well-being, and flourishing in a good life, the relations between these aspects, and the extent to which they depend on luck and social considerations.

courses.name: Philosophy and Popular Culture
courses.description: Philosophy is all around us, it permeates culture. This course explores philosophical questions through the lens of popular culture. The material used may include films, shows, music, novels, video games, advertising, comic books, and so on.

courses.name: Introduction to Environmental Ethics
courses.description: Major questions in environmental ethics: How should human beings view their relationship to the rest of nature? Is responsible stewardship of the environment compatible with current technology? Must future generations be protected? Do animals, other life forms, endangered species, ecosystems and/or the biosphere have value/rights?.

courses.name: Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
courses.description: AI and robotic technologies raise pressing ethical issues. Topics discussed may include big data and privacy, the ‘black box’ problem and bias, human-AI interaction, automation and responsibility, AI codes of ethics, the moral status of AI, and potential risks to humanity posed by superintelligence.

courses.name: Philosophy of the Paranormal
courses.description: Examination of claims, concepts, theories and methods in parapsychology. Their scientific character and the relation of paranormal phenomena to philosophical issues such as survival of death, human nature, time, space, causality and perception.

courses.name: Bioethics
courses.description: Ethical and political issues in medicine, public health, biotechnology, and the life sciences. Topics may include reproductive ethics, research on human subjects, animal research and treatment, justice and health care, physician-patient relationships, death and the end of life, and genetic engineering.

courses.name: Introduction to Philosophy of Mind
courses.description: An introduction to major philosophical issues concerning human cognition. Topics may include: the relation of mind to body, knowledge of other minds, the relation of mental states to personhood and personal identity, mental illness, consciousness, intentionality, action, mental realism.

courses.name: Language and Communication
courses.description: Some of the central topics in the study of language and communication as pursued by linguists and philosophers. The nature of meaning; the connections between language, communication and cognition; language as a social activity.

courses.name: Introduction to Philosophical Logic
courses.description: An introduction to features of rational thinking activity, its expression, and its relation to the world, focusing on such topics as predication, truth, negation, necessity, entailment, logical form, or quantification.

courses.name: Personal Identity and the Self
courses.description: Philosophical perspectives on personal identity, the self, and the underlying issue of the relationship of the mind to the body. Both philosophical and psychological concepts of identity are discussed, as are related issues such as memory, introspection, and self-knowledge.

courses.name: Moral Psychology
courses.description: An examination of psychological underpinnings of morality, focusing on studies at the intersection of philosophy, psychiatry, and psychology.

courses.name: Philosophy of Religion
courses.description: A study of philosophical issues arising from religion. Topics may include: arguments for and against the existence of God, religious experience, death and the afterlife, miracles, God and evil, the relationship between religion and science, and the relationship between religion and ethics.

courses.name: Asian Philosophy
courses.description: An examination of South Asian and East Asian philosophical texts, from the period of the Upanishads and early Buddhism in India to modern philosophical movements. Historical sources may include Hindu, Buddhist, Jain, Confucian or Taoist texts, with a focus on metaphysical, epistemological or ethical themes.

courses.name: Philosophy of Art
courses.description: Philosophical approaches to the study of art. Topics such as: the nature of art and artistic value; representation and symbolism in art; art and artifice; art and the emotions; art, culture and ideology; post-structuralism and art; theories of creativity; relationship between artworks and audiences.

courses.name: Truth and Propaganda
courses.description: Ancient and modern techniques of persuasion from analytical, ethical and jurisprudential perspectives.  Objectivity and bias, advertising and public relations ethics, the viability of democracy in the light of pressures on and within the modern mass media.

courses.name: Topics in Ancient Philosophy
courses.description: A study of philosophers, texts, problems and issues in ancient philosophy, generally with a focus on Plato and Aristotle.

courses.name: Early Greek Philosophy
courses.description: A study of the pre-Socratic Greek philosophers and of the Sophists and Socrates.

courses.name: 17th Century Philosophy
courses.description: European philosophy of the 17 th  century. Representative works of writers such as Francis Bacon, Descartes, Spinoza, Leibniz, and Locke.

courses.name: 18th Century Philosophy
courses.description: European philosophy of the 18 th  century. Representative works of writers such as Berkeley, Hume, and Kant.

courses.name: 19th Century Philosophy
courses.description: European philosophy in the 19 th  century. May include Hegel, Marx, Schopenhauer, Kierkegaard, Nietzsche, Mill.

courses.name: Topics in European Philosophy
courses.description: A study of philosophers, texts, problems and issues in any period of European philosophy.

courses.name: Special Topics in Global Philosophical Traditions
courses.description: A study of philosophers, texts, and doctrines beyond the Western tradition. Traditions covered will vary but may include Asian, African, Muslim or Indigenous philosophy, possibly with critical comparison to Western counterparts.

courses.name: Philosophy of Law: The Logic of Law
courses.description: Legal reasoning and analysis of concepts of particular significance to the law, including justice, rights and duties, liability, punishment, ownership and possession.

courses.name: The Roots of Analytic Philosophy
courses.description: In the context of the work of such writers as Frege and Bradley, a discussion of early philosophical works of Russell, Moore and Wittgenstein. In addition some early representatives of positivism and pragmatism may be examined.

courses.name: Epistemology
courses.description: Fundamental issues concerning the relation between evidence, rationality, and knowledge. Topics may include: skepticism, the nature of belief, the structure of justification, the relative contributions of reason and sense experience to knowledge, innate knowledge, the problem of induction, and the knowledge of other minds.

courses.name: Metaphysics
courses.description: Philosophical issues concerning the fundamental nature of being. Topics may include: time and temporality, space, substance, universals/particulars, identity, causation, freedom/determinism, the nature of norms.

courses.name: Issues in the Philosophy of Science
courses.description: Selected topic(s) in the philosophy of science, such as its relationship to values, or in the philosophy of a particular science (such as philosophy of mathematics, philosophy of physics, philosophy of biology, and philosophy of the social sciences).

courses.name: Symbolic Logic
courses.description: A review of the basic techniques of propositional and predicate logic. Natural deduction and consistency trees. Soundness and completeness. Alternative semantics. Extensions to basic logic: identity, modal logic with possible world semantics, three valued systems, deontic logic.

courses.name: Contemporary Ethical Theory
courses.description: Critical study of modern ethical theories, their views on the nature of morality and the justification of moral claims. Topics may include utilitarianism, libertarianism, communitarianism, egoism, neo-Kantianism, virtue ethics, social contract ethics, feminist ethics, and moral rights.

courses.name: Topics in History of Social and Political Philosophy
courses.description: A critical examination of selected topics and perspectives in the history of social and political philosophy.

courses.name: Topics in Contemporary Social and Political Philosophy
courses.description: A critical examination of some contemporary approaches to topics in social and political philosophy, such as liberalism, feminism, contractarianism, Marxism, libertarianism, and communitarianism.

courses.name: Philosophy, Ethics, and Public Affairs
courses.description: Advanced study of a set of public policy issues, a particular theory or group of theories, or a particular philosopher, concerning philosophical and ethical aspects of public affairs.

courses.name: Philosophy, Economics, and Public Policy
courses.description: The course explores issues at the intersection of philosophy and economics, with a special focus on socially and politically relevant issues. Topics may include: efficiency, cooperation, equity and distributive justice, commodification and the moral limits of markets.

courses.name: Environments, Technology and Values
courses.description: Advanced treatment of ethical issues concerning technologies and environments, including: sustainable development, women and the environment, biological diversity, intrinsic or natural value or rights of non-humans, humans' relation to the rest of the natural world, obligations to future generations, liberty versus equality.

courses.name: Topics in Aesthetics
courses.description: Topics may include theories of aesthetic norms and valuation from ancient Greece onward, or applications of aesthetic theory to various genres of art.

courses.name: Philosophy of Cognitive Science
courses.description: Philosophical issues arising from cognitive science. Topics may include: the proper methodology for studying the mind, the very possibility of a “science of mind”, the computer model of the mind and reactions to it.

courses.name: Mind and Action
courses.description: Philosophical thought concerning the relation between mentality and agency. Topics may include: the relation between belief, desire, and behaviour; rationality and normativity; representing and doing; subjectivity and intersubjectivity; physical and psychological laws; mental causation. Authors may include: Wittgenstein, Heidegger, Ryle, Sellars, Anscombe, Davidson, Taylor, McDowell.

courses.name: Artificial Intelligence: Philosophical and Ethical Issues
courses.description: Topics examined through the lens of philosophy and cognitive science may include humans’ obligations towards AI, sentient AI, implications of AI for models of cognition, designing ethical AI systems, implications of using AI in healthcare, and social inequality and job displacement related to AI.

courses.name: Pragmatics
courses.description: The study of language use in its conversational and cultural contexts. Topics include: conversational implicature; deixis; the semantics-pragmatics boundary; speaker's reference; speech acts. May include cross-cultural pragmatics.

courses.name: Semantics
courses.description: Study of language meaning. Lexical meaning and meanings of larger linguistic expressions, including nominal units, verbal units, and sentences. Meaning relationships between utterances. Relationship between linguistic meaning (semantics) and contextual meaning (pragmatics). Basic formal treatments of semantics.

courses.name: Philosophy of Language
courses.description: An intensive introduction to philosophy of language. Topics may include meaning, reference and truth, speech acts, the nature of concepts, language learning, metaphor, compositionality, context-sensitivity.

courses.name: Philosophy of Emotions
courses.description: Emotions are central to human experience and widely studied in philosophy and science. In order to better understand them and their role in our lives, this course explores philosophical questions about emotions as they arise in philosophy of mind and cognitive science, ethics, and aesthetics.

courses.name: Independent Study
courses.description: Essays and/or examinations based on a list of readings provided by the instructor.

courses.name: Independent Study
courses.description: Essays and/or examinations based on a list of readings provided by the instructor.

courses.name: Independent Study
courses.description: Essays and/or examinations based on a list of readings provided by the instructor.

courses.name: Independent Study
courses.description: Essays and/or examinations based on a bibliography constructed by the student in consultation with the instructor.

courses.name: Independent Study
courses.description: Essays and/or examinations based on a bibliography constructed by the student in consultation with the instructor.

courses.name: Independent Study
courses.description: Essays and/or examinations based on a bibliography constructed by the student in consultation with the instructor.

courses.name: Seminar in philosophy Before the Modern Period
courses.description: Detailed study of selected philosophers or issues in philosophy before the modern period.

courses.name: Seminar in philosophy Before the Modern Period
courses.description: Detailed study of selected philosophers or issues in philosophy before the modern period.

courses.name: Seminar in Modern Philosophy
courses.description: Detailed study of selected philosophers or issues in modern philosophy.

courses.name: Seminar in Modern Philosophy
courses.description: Detailed study of selected philosophers or issues in modern philosophy.

courses.name: Seminar in Contemporary Philosophy
courses.description: Detailed study of selected philosophers or issues in contemporary philosophy.

courses.name: Seminar in Contemporary Philosophy
courses.description: Detailed study of selected philosophers or issues in contemporary philosophy.

courses.name: Lexical Semantics
courses.description: Study of the meaning of words. Topics may include lexical decomposition, meaning variation, lexical relations, and lexical aspect.

courses.name: Special Topic
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in philosophy.

courses.name: Seminar in Philosophy of Language or Linguistics
courses.description: Detailed study of selected issues or the work of selected philosophers in philosophy of language or on philosophical topics in linguistics.

courses.name: Seminar in philosophy of Mind or Cognition
courses.description: Detailed study of selected issues or the work of selected philosophers in philosophy of mind or philosophical aspects of cognition.

courses.name: Seminar in Metaphysics, Epistemology, or Philosophy of Science
courses.description: Detailed study of selected issues or the work of selected philosophers in metaphysics, epistemology, or philosophy of science.

courses.name: Seminar in Ethical Theory or Meta-Ethics
courses.description: Detailed study of selected issues pertaining to ethical theory or issues of meta-ethics such as realism, relativism, moral knowledge.

courses.name: Seminar in Ethics or Moral Philosophy
courses.description: Detailed study of selected issues in ethics or moral philosophy.

courses.name: Seminar in Social or Political Philosophy
courses.description: Detailed study of selected issues in social or political philosophy.

courses.name: Special Topic in Applied Ethics
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in applied ethics.

courses.name: Special Topic in Applied Ethics
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in applied ethics.

courses.name: Special Topic in Aesthetics or Philosophy of Art
courses.description: Detailed study of a special issue or a single author in aesthetics and/or philosophy of art.

courses.name: Special Topic in Aesthetics or Philosophy of Art
courses.description: Detailed study of a special issue or a single author in aesthetics and/or philosophy of art.

courses.name: Special Topic in Philosophy of Law
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in philosophy of law.

courses.name: Special Topic in Philosophy of Law
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in philosophy of law.

courses.name: Special Topic in Philosophy of Computing
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in philosophy of computing.

courses.name: Special Topic in Philosophy of Computing
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in philosophy of computing.

courses.name: Formal Semantics
courses.description: Advanced topics in compositional semantics and its interfaces. Topics may include: logic, semantic types, lambda calculus, intentional contexts, possible world semantics, interfaces with syntax and pragmatics quantification, anaphora, presupposition, implicatures, scope and binding, and model theory.

courses.name: Special Topic in Feminist Philosophy
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in feminist philosophy.

courses.name: Special Topic in Feminist Philosophy
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in feminist philosophy.

courses.name: Special Topic in Logic
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in Logic.

courses.name: Special Topic in Logic
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in Logic.

courses.name: Special Topic in Philosophical Logic
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in Philosophical Logic.

courses.name: Special Topic in Philosophical Logic
courses.description: Detailed study of a special topic in Philosophical Logic.

free_electives.degree: Philosophy with Concentration in Philosophy, Ethics and Public Affairs B.A. Honours (20.0 credits)

free_electives.text: 9. Sufficient free electives to make 20.0 credits in total for the program
